caveats: a: The occupation mix is a broader four-digit proxy and does not isolate paint stores.
a: Sherwin-Williams is a vertically integrated large-chain proxy excluded from the target population.
a: Product and color advice may be technically exposed but still require accountable human confirmation.
rho: BTOS measures adoption, not labor implementation depth or savings.
rho: Retail Trade is much broader than specialty paint retail.
rho: Large manufacturer-owned chains may receive tools earlier than LMM independents.
e: The firm count is margin-bridged from receipts using a broader retail EBITDA margin and may misclassify firms.
e: Embedded services are rarely reported separately from merchandise sales.
e: The largest visible specialty-store network is manufacturer-owned and outside the acquisition lens.
s5: Gallup spans all industries and measures plans rather than completed control changes.
s5: The transaction source combines building-material and hardware stores and does not isolate paint retail.
s5: Independent paint retailers may face buyer and supplier constraints not present in other retail categories.
q: No source directly measures AI benefit pass-through in independent paint retail.
q: Capture differs between national brands, private label, contractor pricing, consumer purchases, and delivery.
q: The estimate excludes demand effects and implementation failure.
d5: The coatings outlook is short-horizon and covers manufacturers and channels outside 444120.
d5: Painter employment is an indirect demand indicator and excludes much DIY activity.
d5: Paint volume does not directly measure retailer advisory, tinting, delivery, or account-service quantity.
o: Retail-sales requirements are not paint-store-specific or forward-looking.
o: Operator-required quantity is distinct from labor hours per order.
o: Wallpaper and decorative consumer sales may shift online faster than professional coatings demand.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Primarily transaction-based paint, coating, wallpaper, and accessory sales, with repeat professional accounts and recurring repaint cycles; advisory, tinting, delivery, and account services are usually embedded in product margin rather than separately billed.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturer-owned national chains, captive distribution branches, pure online marketplaces, passive entities, one-off product sellers without a recurring service relationship, painting contractors classified elsewhere, and stores without transferable operations or customer accounts.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market paint and wallpaper retailers that repeatedly serve external painting contractors, property managers, commercial accounts, and homeowners through product selection, color advice and matching, tinting, quote and account service, trade credit, order fulfillment, and local delivery.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for the broader Building Material and Supplies Dealers industry, not 444120. I treat routine color and product guidance, takeoffs and quantity calculation, quotes, account service, order entry, delivery scheduling, purchasing, marketing, bookkeeping, and management reporting as partly AI-exposed, while physical tinting, matching verification, stocking, lifting, loading, delivery, and complex specification judgment are less substitutable; broader home-center employment likely overweights cashiers and material movers relative to specialty paint stores, making the net error direction uncertain.
caveats: The occupation mix is a broader four-digit proxy and does not isolate paint stores.
Sherwin-Williams is a vertically integrated large-chain proxy excluded from the target population.
Product and color advice may be technically exposed but still require accountable human confirmation.
a.change_evidence: A 444120-specific payroll-by-occupation extract plus time-and-motion studies for counter sales, color matching, tinting, outside sales, delivery, credit, purchasing, and back office, net of current point-of-sale and color-system automation.
a.rationale: The broader industry combines very large retail-sales and cashier populations with substantial stocking and material-handling employment. Paint stores add information-rich product, color, and contractor-account workflows that AI can assist, but frequent customer interaction and physical preparation and fulfillment keep the wage-weighted opportunity to a minority of work.

d5.bridge: The American Coatings Association forecast measures U.S. architectural-coatings volume, including channels beyond paint retailers, and BLS projects painter employment rather than constant-quality retail service demand. I translate modest coatings-volume and painter growth into broadly stable-to-slightly-higher store demand, then widen for construction cycles, DIY shifts, repaint intervals, manufacturer-direct sales, and channel share; coatings forecasts may overstate retailer quantity if other channels gain share.
caveats: The coatings outlook is short-horizon and covers manufacturers and channels outside 444120.
Painter employment is an indirect demand indicator and excludes much DIY activity.
Paint volume does not directly measure retailer advisory, tinting, delivery, or account-service quantity.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-volume architectural-coatings forecast by independent paint store, manufacturer-owned store, home-center, e-commerce, and direct channels, split between professional repaint, new construction, maintenance, and DIY.
d5.rationale: ACA estimated architectural-paint volume down 2.5% in 2025 but forecast a 1.7% rebound in 2026. BLS projects painter employment to rise 4% from 2024 to 2034 and notes recurring demand from construction and property sale or lease. Repainting and maintenance provide a durable base, but current weakness and channel competition argue against strong five-year growth.

caveats: The firm count is margin-bridged from receipts using a broader retail EBITDA margin and may misclassify firms.
Embedded services are rarely reported separately from merchandise sales.
The largest visible specialty-store network is manufacturer-owned and outside the acquisition lens.
e.change_evidence: A review of the 132 dataset-inferred LMM firms using ownership, normalized EBITDA, professional-account share, service activity, delivery, trade credit, repeat revenue, manufacturer affiliation, customer concentration, and owner dependence.
e.rationale: The NAICS is defined as retailing paint, wallpaper, and related supplies, so product resale alone does not meet the recurring outsourced-service lens. A meaningful minority should qualify through durable professional accounts, tinting and matching, trade credit, delivery, specification support, and repeat property-maintenance demand, while manufacturer-owned stores, micro-retailers, one-off consumer shops, and owner-dependent firms are ineligible.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 444120 ($2,411,227,634) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 444120 ($15,773,299,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1421 (margins.json[444]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Building Supply) EBITDA/Sales 14.21%); revenue/employee $470,480 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 444120); class <5: 795 firms, avg revenue/firm $668,784 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $95,034 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 304 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,868,826 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $265,560 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 144 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,904,389 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $554,814 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 99 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,438,949 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,341,275 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 33 firms, avg revenue/firm $40,214,030 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,714,414 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,316,638,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $187,094,260 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 132

o.bridge: BLS ORS measures current requirements for retail salespersons, and Sherwin-Williams describes a current large-chain service model, rather than the year-five share of eligible paint-retail quantity needing an accountable operator. Frequent external interaction, store-based contractor service, physical tinting and fulfillment, trade credit, and inventory support persistence, while online ordering, visualization, automated advice, and direct delivery can self-serve some demand; the large-chain proxy may overstate both automation and service sophistication for LMM firms.
caveats: Retail-sales requirements are not paint-store-specific or forward-looking.
Operator-required quantity is distinct from labor hours per order.
Wallpaper and decorative consumer sales may shift online faster than professional coatings demand.
o.change_evidence: Channel and workflow evidence on online-only paint sales, automated tinting and matching, direct-to-jobsite manufacturer fulfillment, error and return rates, professional-account migration, store closures, and remotely operated retail formats.
o.rationale: Customers can increasingly choose colors, estimate quantities, and order online, but accurate tinting, color matching, specification, urgent pickup, hazardous-material handling, delivery exceptions, returns, trade credit, and professional-account accountability still require an operating retailer over five years.

caveats: No source directly measures AI benefit pass-through in independent paint retail.
Capture differs between national brands, private label, contractor pricing, consumer purchases, and delivery.
The estimate excludes demand effects and implementation failure.
q.change_evidence: Matched store-level pre/post deployment data on gross margin, realized prices, labor hours, quote conversion, repeat professional-account spend, delivery economics, inventory losses, bad debt, and customer churn.
q.rationale: Operators can retain benefit through faster contractor service, more accurate ordering, improved follow-up, lower administrative hiring, better delivery routing, reduced stockouts, and tighter credit control. Branded-product price comparison, negotiated contractor discounts, manufacturer-owned chain competition, and easy diffusion of customer-service tools force sharing, while color history, urgent jobsite needs, trusted advice, trade credit, and local availability provide some stickiness.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures use of AI in any business function across Retail Trade, not the implemented share of exposed paint-store work over five years. Current retail use near 14% and six-month expected use near 17% anchor present adoption; I allow diffusion of packaged product search, quoting, color visualization, account service, forecasting, scheduling, and administration, but discount for LMM system fragmentation, training, hazardous-product rules, color accuracy, and customer trust.
caveats: BTOS measures adoption, not labor implementation depth or savings.
Retail Trade is much broader than specialty paint retail.
Large manufacturer-owned chains may receive tools earlier than LMM independents.
rho.change_evidence: Independent-dealer cohorts from paint-industry software vendors showing feature deployment, active usage, override and error rates, training time, labor hours avoided, and persistence after rollout.
rho.rationale: Many exposed workflows are structured and can be added around existing point-of-sale, customer, and tinting systems. Implementation should outpace full physical automation, yet low current retail adoption and the need to integrate customer history, product specifications, inventory, pricing, and credit limit the five-year share.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale or transfer plans among U.S. business owners, including 22% of employer owners, rather than completed qualifying control transfers of eligible paint retailers. I trim for closures, failed or deferred sales, internal transfers, and manufacturer roll-ins, while observed building-material and hardware transactions and unplanned exits partly offset that trim; intention data likely overstates completions and marketplace reports undercount private deals.
caveats: Gallup spans all industries and measures plans rather than completed control changes.
The transaction source combines building-material and hardware stores and does not isolate paint retail.
Independent paint retailers may face buyer and supplier constraints not present in other retail categories.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year longitudinal deal data for 444120 firms, including the eligible denominator, listings, completed control transfers, manufacturer acquisitions, family transfers, closures, failed deals, and seller age.
s5.rationale: Aging ownership and an active adjacent retail transaction market support transfer supply. Paint stores can be transferable when contractor accounts, staff, supplier access, tinting equipment, and delivery processes survive the owner, but manufacturer concentration, customer relationships, inventory, leases, credit exposure, and affiliation agreements narrow qualifying outcomes.

principal_driver: Repeat professional accounts create information-rich quoting, color, order, credit, inventory, and delivery workflows that can be improved without automating the physical product.
principal_weakness: Product retail dominates the NAICS, and manufacturer-owned chains constrain both eligible-firm availability and competitive value capture.
